---
title: "Corruption & Wealth"
author: "Huong Tong | Project 2"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    embed-resources: true
    scrollable: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: true
---

## The Question

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](https://raketcontent.com/20240108_131242_0000_5015e25f5f.png){width="100%"}
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Why are some countries rich and some poor?

One thing kept showing up in the data above everything else.

**How corrupt the government is.**

-   **CPI score** — 0 = extremely corrupt, 100 = very clean
-   **GDP per capita** — economic output per person, in USD
:::
:::::

## The Data

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false

library(tidyverse)
library(scales)

gdp_raw <- read_csv("API_NY.GDP.PCAP.CD_DS2_en_csv_v2_121663.csv", skip = 4)
cpi_raw <- read_csv("global-cpi-all.csv")
```

Two datasets. Both public. Both free.

|   | CPI Dataset | GDP Dataset |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Source** | Transparency International | World Bank |
| **Coverage** | 180 countries, 2012–2025 | 266 countries, 1960–2024 |
| **Key column** | `score` — 0 (corrupt) to 100 (clean) | `gdp_pc` — USD per person |
| **Joined by** | `iso3` country code + `year` | → **178 countries, 2,067 rows** |

## Reshaping the Data

```{r}
#| label: wrangle
#| echo: true

# GDP file has years as columns — pivot_longer() fixes that
gdp_long <- gdp_raw |>
  select(`Country Name`, `Country Code`, `2012`:`2023`) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = `2012`:`2023`,
    names_to  = "year",       # column names → "year" column
    values_to = "gdp_pc"      # values → "gdp_pc" column
  ) |>
  mutate(year = as.integer(year)) |>
  filter(!is.na(gdp_pc)) |>
  rename(iso3 = `Country Code`)

# inner_join() — only keeps countries in BOTH datasets
df <- inner_join(
  gdp_long,
  cpi_raw |> select(iso3, country, region, year, cpi = score),
  by = c("iso3", "year")
) |>
  filter(gdp_pc > 0) |>
  mutate(log_gdp = log10(gdp_pc))

cat("Countries:", n_distinct(df$iso3), "| Rows:", nrow(df))
```

## Who's in the Data?

```{r}
#| label: first-look
#| echo: true

df |>
  filter(year == 2022) |>
  arrange(desc(cpi)) |>
  select(Country = country,
         Region  = region,
         `CPI Score` = cpi,
         `GDP per capita` = gdp_pc) |>
  slice(c(1:3, (n() - 2):n())) |>
  mutate(`GDP per capita` = dollar(`GDP per capita`, accuracy = 1)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Top 3 least corrupt vs 3 most corrupt countries, 2022")
```

Denmark (CPI 90) — **\$67,781** per person. Somalia (CPI 12) — **\$573** per person. Same planet. Same year.

## The Global Pattern (2022)

```{r}
#| label: scatter
#| echo: true
#| output-location: slide
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

label_these <- c("Denmark", "Finland", "Singapore",
                 "Somalia", "Venezuela", "Syria",
                 "Russia", "Brazil", "Rwanda")

df_2022 <- df |> filter(year == 2022)

ggplot(df_2022, aes(x = cpi, y = log_gdp, color = region)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm",
              color = "black", linewidth = 1, se = TRUE) +
  geom_text(
    data  = df_2022 |> filter(country %in% label_these),
    aes(label = country),
    size = 3, color = "black", vjust = -0.8
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0("$", round(10^x / 1e3, 0), "k")
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(10, 90, 10)) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Region") +
  labs(
    title    = "Less corruption → higher GDP per capita",
    subtitle = "178 countries, 2022. Black line = linear regression.",
    x = "CPI Score (0 = very corrupt, 100 = very clean)",
    y = "GDP per capita"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## Before We Look at the Numbers...

![](inequality.jpg){width="80%" fig-align="center"}

> *Same city. Same morning. Different worlds. Why?*

## The Wealth Gap

```{r}
#| label: gap
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

df |>
  filter(year == 2022) |>
  mutate(
    cpi_group = cut(cpi,
      breaks = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100),
      labels = c("Very Corrupt\n(0–25)", "Corrupt\n(26–50)",
                 "Clean\n(51–75)", "Very Clean\n(76–100)"),
      include.lowest = TRUE)
  ) |>
  group_by(cpi_group) |>
  summarise(median_gdp = median(gdp_pc), n = n()) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cpi_group, y = median_gdp, fill = cpi_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(dollar(median_gdp, accuracy = 1),
                               "\n(", n, " countries)")),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 4.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("#d73027", "#fc8d59", "#91bfdb", "#4575b4")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = dollar,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Median GDP per capita by corruption level (2022)",
    subtitle = "Very Clean countries are 27× richer than Very Corrupt ones",
    x = NULL, y = "Median GDP per capita"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

Wealth gap is **27 times** more.

## So How Strong Is This Really?

```{r}
#| label: interpretation
#| echo: false

model <- lm(log_gdp ~ cpi, data = df |> filter(year == 2022))
b   <- round(coef(model)["cpi"], 4)
pct <- round((10^b - 1) * 100, 1)
r2  <- round(summary(model)$r.squared * 100, 1)
```

We ran one line of R on 178 countries.

```r
lm(log_gdp ~ cpi, data = df)
```

. . .

Out of every reason a country could be rich or poor —
geography, history, trade, education, resources —

**corruption score alone accounts for `r r2`% of it.**

. . .

And every single point a country cleans up —

**`r pct`% more money in the pocket of the average person.**

That's schools getting funded.
Hospitals getting built.
Kids getting a shot they wouldn't have had.

. . .

------------------------------------------------------------------------

In 2024 — Acemoglu, Johnson & Robinson won the Nobel Prize in Economics.

A lifetime of research. Their conclusion —

. . .

*"What separates rich countries from poor countries*
*is whether institutions work for the people — or extract from them."*

. . .


And our data — 178 countries, 12 years —
points exactly in the same direction.

. . .

## What We Found

- **178 countries, 12 years** of real data
- CPI alone explains **62%** of why some countries are rich and others aren't
- Very clean countries are **27× richer** than very corrupt ones
- **The cause is still open — does corruption create poverty, or does poverty create corruption?**
